steps.text: Use a heated head pump that is constructed from materials non-reactive with the analytes of interest.

preconditions.text: The pump is positioned upstream of the distribution manifold and FTIR system.

constraints.text: The sampling probe must be capable of sustained heating to prevent water condensation and adsorption of analytes.

steps.text: Collect reference spectra.
Determine the absorption cell path length.
Determine instrument resolution.
Verify that there are no significant vacuum-side leaks using either the low-flow test or the vacuum-decay test.
Verify the gas cell pathlength according to the procedures in section 10.6.4.
Perform the QA spike procedure.
Perform sampling procedures, including performing a stratification check.
Compare the pre- and post-test CTS.

preconditions.text: The method has been validated for each of the target analytes at the source.

constraints.text: For the purposes of this method, the CTS must be ethylene, methane, or carbon dioxide. Other compounds may be used only with administrator approval.

steps.text: Ensure safety data sheets (SDS) for gas standards are available to all personnel using this method.
Ensure that gases are properly vented.
Ensure that the gas handling system is leak free.

constraints.text: Exposure to HAPs (suspected toxic or hazardous compounds) from stack gas or from spiking standards should be avoided.
Follow safety and health practices consistent with stationary source sampling, including applicable legal and site-specific safety requirements.

steps.text: Calculate the SAR via Equation 3.
Calculate the effective spike addition (ESA) using Equation 4.
Calculate the spike recovery (SR).

preconditions.text: When using a non-certified cylinder, replace the Cspike term in Equation 4 with MCspiked.

constraints.text: Use consistent concentration units for each relevant variable in Equation 3.
Use consistent concentration units for each relevant variable in Equation 4.
For the section 11.4 QA spike, the ESA must be within 50% of the native stack concentration.

steps.text: Flow the CTS through the FTIR cell.
Record a set of the absorption spectra of the CTS, and record the temperature, pressure, and concentration of the CTS.
Record the instrument manufacturer’s nominal absorption pathlength, nominal spectral resolution, and the CTS signal integration period.
Calculate the reference cell absorption pathlength according to the provided equation.
Flow ambient air through the gas cell.
Verify the instrument resolution using a water absorbance peak near either 1,918 cm-1, 3,050 cm-1, or 3,920 cm-1.
Mix additional humified air or nitrogen with the ambient flow to achieve an absorbance of approximately 0.25 absorbance units.
Record an absorbance spectrum and measure the FWHH of the chosen water peak.
Verify that there are no significant vacuum-side leaks using one of the leak tests.

preconditions.text: The absorbance of two consecutive spectra must differ by less than or equal to the uncertainty of the cylinder standard.
The measured FWHH of the water peak must be within 5% of the nominal instrument resolution.

constraints.text: The CTS gas must be one of the following gases: ethylene, methane, or carbon dioxide.

steps.text: Seal the probe end of the system as close to the probe opening as possible by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Operate all vacuum pumps.
Draw a vacuum on the sampling system and let the pressure on the system stabilize.
Turn off the sample pumps and seal the system under a vacuum of 250 mmHg greater than the source static pressure.
Record the absolute pressure and the system absolute temperature every 30 seconds for 5 minutes.
Observe the single beam instrument response in the frequency region below the detector cutoff (usually <400 cm¥1), where the detector response is known to be zero.
Verify that the detector response is ‘‘flat’’ and equal to zero in this region, or at least 100 times less than the peak signal in the entire spectrum.
Decrease the aperture or attenuate the IR beam, and repeat the linearity check until the detector response is linear.
Verify the gas cell pathlength of your instrument by following the procedure found in section 10.6.4 of this method.
Choose one of two options and perform the standard addition procedure listed in ection 9.3 of this method.
Use a certified standard (±2% accuracy) for an analyte that has been validated at the source.

preconditions.text: If the response is not linear
For unstable sources, QA spiking may be difficult. An alternative procedure for such a source is described in section 9.3.5.4.

constraints.text: The leak rate must be equal to or less than 2.5 mmHg per minute.

steps.text: Use a non-certified cylinder for an analyte that has been validated at the source (QA Spike Option 2).
Perform sampling using a continuous flow of source gas.
Use a probe of appropriate length to measure the analyte of interest at each of 12 traverse points (or nine points for a rectangular stack).
Calculate the mean measured concentration for all sampling points (MNavg).
Calculate the percent stratification (St) of each traverse point.

preconditions.text: If the spike recovery (Option 1), as calculated by Equation 5 of this method, is within 70–130%
If the spike recovery (Option 2), as calculated by equation 5 of this method, is within 90–110%
If the concentration at each traverse point differs from the mean [by the specified limit]

constraints.text: An appropriate surrogate must have a vapor pressure that is less than or equal to the analyte of interest and be less soluble in water.
The wavelength at which the surrogate is to be quantified must be reported and be within 100 wavenumbers of a wavenumber that will be used to quantify the analyte of interest.
The pKa of a surrogate must be within 20% of the pKa of the analyte of interest.
Surrogates are not allowed for the following analytes: formaldehyde, HCl, HF, NH3, and vinyl chloride.

steps.text: Electronically store all field test data, ensuring it is readily available.
Provide stored data to the regulatory authority upon request.
Measure analyte concentrations using reference spectra as described in section 10.5.
Calculate the concentration of each species in the sample matrix and their respective residuals using the algorithm developed in section 8.3.
Perform correction for differences in gas cell pathlength, temperature, and cell pressure using equation 12.

preconditions.text: If the algorithm does not have the capability to correct for differences in gas cell pathlength, temperature, and cell pressure between sample and reference spectra.

constraints.text: Stored information must include: sample interferograms, background interferograms, CTS sample interferograms, processed sample absorbance spectra, and processed CTS absorbance spectra.
The DL for this method must be less than or equal to 20% of the applicable compliance limit for the compound being measured.
